title: Layered pulp colour effects
subtitle: Using two or more coloured pulps to create patterned sheets
excerpt: Layered and blended pulp techniques produce sheets with marbled, clouded, and watermarked colour effects baked into the paper itself. The colour is not applied to the surface; it is formed as the sheet is pulled.
sourceNotes: Coloured-pulp papermaking techniques are synthesised from general hand-papermaking practice. The use of natural pigments and dyes in Western papermaking is documented in Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), public domain, archive.org/details/oldpapermaking00hunt.

glossaryTerms.definition: In traditional hand papermaking, the worker who pulls the sheet from the vat. A single-person home studio fills both the vatman and coucher roles.

Applying concentrated coloured pulp to a freshly pulled base sheet before the water has drained, using a brush, ladle, or squeeze bottle. The applied pulp bonds with the base sheet as both dry together.

content.text: Layered pulp work combines two or more coloured pulp batches to produce a sheet with pattern or colour variation built into its structure. The effects range from subtle (a fleck of contrasting colour in the vat) to graphic (patches of intense colour applied to the wet sheet while it is still on the mould). The
controls the layering at the moment of pulling.

attrs.title: Safety: blender use near water
attrs.body: Keep the blender unplugged when charging it with fibre or rinsing out. Drain the vat before reaching in with both hands.

items.name: Two or more batches of prepared pulp
items.description: Each batch blended and tinted separately. Colour with plant-based dyes, natural earth pigments, or coloured tissue paper blended into the pulp batch.

items.name: Papermaking vat (slug: papermaking-vat)
items.description: One main vat for the base pulp; a second bowl or bucket for the accent pulp.

items.name: A ladle, large spoon, or squeeze bottle
items.description: For applying the accent pulp to the wet base sheet.

content.text: The simplest method adds a small quantity of contrasting pulp directly to the vat. Stir both pulps together briefly: thorough mixing produces a uniform tint; minimal mixing gives distinct flecks. Each sheet pulled will carry the pattern from the blended vat. Stir before each pull as the pulps settle at different rates.

Pull a base sheet from the main vat in the standard way. While the base sheet is still on the mould and draining, use a ladle to pour a small quantity of the accent pulp in a strip or patch across the wet base sheet. Tilt the mould gently to let excess water drain. The accent pulp settles against the base sheet and the two layers bond as they dry together.

Couch the base sheet and leave it on the couching cloth, still damp. Use a spoon or a brush to apply small amounts of concentrated accent pulp in specific positions on the wet sheet. Press gently with the sponge to ensure the applied pulp bonds rather than sitting proud of the surface. Dry under moderate weight.

Blend coloured tissue paper (100% cotton or linen tissue, not wood-pulp tissue, which yellows) directly with the pulp batch to introduce consistent colour throughout the fibres. Alternatively, add a small amount of fabric dye to the blending water. Concentrated plant-based dyes (saffron, beetroot, weld) produce warm earth tones that are variable across the sheet and fade gently over time.
